Title: Two men held over KL shooting, firearm seized
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested two local men in Klang in connection with a shooting incident in the capital, seizing a firearm during the operation.
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said the suspects, aged 27 and 33, were detained at 11.45pm yesterday.
"Police also seized a pistol with a live round, as well as a replica firearm during the arrests," he said in a brief statement.
Fadil said the first suspect has a record involving five prior cases, while the second has 17 prior cases.
"Both suspects have been remanded for seven days beginning April 23 until April 29 to facilitate investigations.
"The case remains under investigation," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran receives first revenue from Strait of Hormuz tolls
Article:
TEHERAN: A senior Iranian parliament official said on Thursday that Tehran has received the first revenue from tolls it imposed on the strategic Strait of Hormuz in its war with the United States and Israel.
"The first revenue received from the Strait of Hormuz tolls was deposited into the Central Bank account," said deputy speaker of parliament Hamidreza Hajibabaei, according to Tasnim news agency.
Other Iranian media carried the same statement, without elaborating.
The Strait of Hormuz, a key energy chokepoint, has become a major flashpoint since the outbreak of the Middle East war on Feb 28.
Iran has allowed only a trickle of ships to pass through the waterway, a route that in peacetime accounts for a fifth of the world's oil and gas flows, along with other vital commodities.
Before the announcement of revenue from tolls, Iran's parliament had been deciding whether to impose them on shipping through the strait, with Iranian officials warning that maritime traffic through the strait would "not return to its pre-war status."
On March 30, Iranian state media said the parliament's security commission had approved plans to impose tolls, but it was not clear if a final parliamentary vote on the proposal had taken place.
US President Donald Trump has been pushing Iran to open the strait.
Britain, France and military planners from over 30 countries have held talks to protect navigation through the strait, with Paris and London saying they would lead a multinational mission as "soon as conditions allow."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC seizes RM18.3mil in properties over NGO fund probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has seized immovable assets worth more than RM18.3 million to assist investigations into the alleged misappropriation of funds involving a non-governmental organisation (NGO).
The commission, in a TikTok post, said the seizure involved 14 properties comprising seven residential units, three office spaces and four plots of land located at various locations.
The total value of the assets seized is estimated at RM18.36 million based on current market valuations.
The anti-graft agency said investigations into the case are ongoing.
MORE TO COME...
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Love, lies and laptops: Police bust love scam syndicate operating out of resort
Article:
TANAH MERAH: Police have busted a love scam syndicate targeting Singaporean men with the arrest of 58 people, all but two of whom are foreigners today.
They were nabbed at a resort here, having taken up several rooms and a hall.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said the 53 men and five women were detained in a 4.30pm operation carried out by the state Commercial Crime Investigation Department.
"Those arrested were people from China, Myanmar, India and Pakistan, as well as two locals.
"Initial investigations revealed that the premises was used as a call centre to carry out online love scam activities targeting victims from Singapore," he told reporters.
Yusoff said the syndicate, believed to have been operating for about a month, used women posing as Indonesians to lure victims.
"The modus operandi involved manipulating the victims' emotions and their focus was Singaporean men," he said.
He added that police seized 46 desktop computers, five laptops, 118 mobile phones and several internet routers, believed to include Starlink devices used to ensure stable and high-speed internet connectivity.
"The total value of the seized items is estimated at RM179,000," he said.
Yusoff said the suspects had been operating daily from noon until midnight, with the syndicate managed by three supervisors.
"Police have yet to estimate the losses suffered by the victims as none of them have filed the report.
"We have also recorded a statement from the resort's operator," he added.
He said 33 of the foreigners entered the country using valid documents while the rest have no documents.
All have been remanded for four days starting today to assist in investigations under Section 420 and Section 120B of the Penal Code.
Yusoff said commercial crime cases in Kelantan had increased by 100 per cent since 2024, and police viewed such online scams seriously.
"We will continue to intensify efforts to combat these syndicates, which not only cause financial losses but also tarnish the country's image internationally," he said.
He urged those with information on illegal call centres or commercial crimes to come forward and assist the police.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hormuz crisis throws spotlight on world's largest 'chokepoint': Strait of Malacca
Article:
THE closure of the Strait of Hormuz has forced policymakers in Asia to face questions over the security of other maritime chokepoints, including the Strait of Malacca, which is the world's busiest waterway for international trade.
WHAT IS THE MALACCA STRAIT?
The 900-km (550-mile) long Malacca Strait, bounded by Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia and Singapore, provides the shortest sea route from East Asia to the Middle East and Europe.
It carries nearly 22 per cent of the world's maritime trade, according to the Centre for Strategic and International Studies.
This includes oil and gas shipments from the Middle East to the energy-hungry economies of China, Japan and South Korea.
Strait of Malacca is the largest "oil transit chokepoint" in the world and the only one that outpaces Hormuz, according to the US Energy Information Administration.
In the first half of 2025, some 23.2 million barrels of oil per day were transported through the Strait of Malacca, accounting for 29 per cent of total maritime oil flows.
The next largest chokepoint, Hormuz, saw about 20.9 million bpd pass through.
More than 102,500 ships, mostly commercial vessels, transited through the Strait of Malacca in 2025, up from around 94,300 in 2024, data from Malaysia's Marine Department showed.
These include most tankers, but some very large vessels avoid the strait because of draught restrictions and go south around Indonesia instead.
This route allows the Strait of Malacca to be bypassed if it were closed, but it adds to journey time that would delay shipments and drive up prices.
WHAT ARE THE CONCERNS ABOUT THE STRAIT OF MALACCA?
At its narrowest point in the Phillips Channel of the Singapore Strait, the Strait of Malacca is only 1.7 miles (2.7 km) wide, creating a natural bottleneck, as well as potential for collisions, grounding, or oil spills.
Some parts of the strait are relatively shallow, with a depth of 25-27 metres (82-90 ft), restricting the largest vessels, but even very large crude carriers (VLCCs) measuring more than 350 metres long, 60 metres wide, and with a draft of more than 20 metres, make the transit.
For years, the strait has been a hotbed of piracy and attacks on merchant vessels.
Last year saw criminal attacks spike to at least 104, but these have fallen off in the first quarter of this year, according to the ReCAAP Information Sharing Centre, an organisation established by regional governments to combat piracy.
The narrow and congested waterway has been strategically important to Beijing, with around 75 per cent of China's seaborne crude oil imports passing through it from the Middle East and Africa, data from tanker tracker Vortexa shows.
The Iran crisis has crystallised long-standing worries about how chokepoints such as Malacca could be affected if a conflict breaks out in the South China Sea or the Taiwan Strait, where another 21 per cent of global maritime trade transits, according to CSIS.
Malaysian authorities say the Strait of Malacca is also a growing spot for illegal ship-to-ship transfers, where oil is shifted between tankers at sea to obscure its origin.
WHAT ARE OFFICIALS SAYING?
Indonesian Finance Minister Purbaya Yudhi Sadewa made waves on Wednesday by openly musing about ways countries could impose tolls on ships as a way to monetise the strait, before noting that such an arrangement is not possible.
When asked about the risks of tolls or other restrictions on movement in the strait, Singapore Foreign Affairs Minister Vivian Balakrishnan told CNBC that the nations along the strait share a strategic interest to keep it open, and have agreed not to collect tolls.
He also said Singapore had assured the United States and China that the right of passage was guaranteed for all and it would not participate in any efforts to block the strait or impose tolls.
Malaysian Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan told a forum on Wednesday that no unilateral decisions can be made about the strait and that Malaysia is on the same page with Singapore, Indonesia and Thailand, and they conduct joint patrols to ensure the waterway remains open.
* The article is by Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor eatery fined RM4k for defying health closure order
Article:
KULAI: An eatery was fined RM4,000 by the magistrate's court here today after being found guilty of defying a closure order issued by health authorities last year.
Magistrate R. Salini imposed the fine after the person in charge of the Indahpura Third Junction Cafe changed his plea to guilty during mention of the case for document submission before trial.
The court also ordered a 4-month prison sentence in default of payment of the fine.
According to the charge, the person in charge of the café was accused of disobeying a closure order for unsanitary food at the premises along Jalan Kenanga 29/4, Taman Indahpura here, at 2.40pm on Feb 23, 2025.
The prosecution was brought under Section 11 of the Food Act 1983, which carries a maximum penalty of five years' imprisonment, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Johor Health Department prosecuting officers Fakhruz Zaman Ma'rof and Muhd Azizul Masri appeared for the prosecution.
Based on the facts of the case, a health officer visited the café on Feb 23 last year to inspect the premises, which had been ordered to close on Feb 21, 2025.
The inspection found that the café was still operating as usual, with staff preparing food for sale, in breach of the written closure order under Section 11 of the Food Act 1983.
Meanwhile, the state health department said the case reflects the ongoing commitment of the Kulai district health office's inspectorate and legal unit to strengthen public health enforcement and ensure compliance among food operators.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar receives courtesy call from Muslim scholars' delegation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today received a courtesy call from a delegation of the International Union of Muslim Scholars, led by its deputy president Muhammad Al-Hasan Al-Diddu Al-Shanqiti, at his office.
In a Facebook post, Anwar said the visit is part of the scholars' programme in Malaysia, aimed at strengthening ties and exchanging views on pressing global issues.
He said discussions during the meeting touched on ongoing conflicts and oppression in several regions, as well as violations of human rights and the sovereignty of nations.
"Various matters were raised, including the crisis of war and oppression, and infringements on the rights and sovereignty of countries," he said.
Anwar added that Muhammad Al-Hasan commended Malaysia's role in fostering unity beyond its borders.
The scholar said that Malaysia is not only safeguarding the welfare of its own people, but has also emerged as a nation capable of bringing together countries and communities of diverse backgrounds in the spirit of peace.
The meeting reflects Malaysia's continued engagement with international scholars and its commitment to promoting moderation, dialogue and global harmony under the Madani framework.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Solarvest bags RM1.06bil solar project from Malakoff
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Solarvest Holdings Bhd has secured a RM1.06 billion contract to develop Malaysia's largest utility-scale solar project under the latest large-scale solar programme.
Through its subsidiary Atlantic Blue Sdn Bhd, Solarvest has been appointed engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning (EPCC) partner and technology provider for the LSS5+ project awarded by Malakoff Corp Bhd via Malakoff Silver Solar Sdn Bhd.
The 690MWp solar photovoltaic plant, located in Larut and Matang, Perak, will span nearly 1,400 acres, roughly equivalent to 1,050 football fields.
Once completed, it is expected to generate up to 970,000 megawatt hours of clean electricity annually, enough to power about 230,000 households.
Commercial operation is targeted for the first quarter of 2028, with the project forming part of Malaysia's broader push to expand renewable capacity and improve long-term energy security amid volatile fuel markets.
Solarvest said the project reflects the growing role of large-scale renewable infrastructure in stabilising the national grid while reducing reliance on conventional energy sources.
"Energy security today is no longer defined solely by the pace of generation growth, but by how efficiently we manage and optimise the energy system," said executive director and group chief executive officer Davis Chong Chun Shiong.
He said grid flexibility, forecasting capability and smart energy management will become increasingly important as renewable penetration rises.
"As renewable energy scales, smart grid technologies will be essential to manage intermittency, and the integration of advanced solutions such as battery energy storage systems, supporting grid modernisation and development of a future-ready power system for Malaysia," he added.
Solarvest said the contract strengthens its orderbook, which now stands at RM2.7 billion, and reinforces its growth trajectory in the regional clean energy sector.
The company expects upcoming opportunities under Malaysia's LSS6 programme, which is anticipated to include greater integration of battery energy storage systems, in line with the National Energy Transition Roadmap.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Armizan: 3 arrested, including 2 Indonesians, over alleged diesel misuse
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Enforcement authorities arrested a local man and two Indonesians for allegedly misusing fleet cards to stockpile diesel illegally at a premises here yesterday.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali said the 2.30am raid was carried out by enforcement personnel following a week of surveillance.
Authorities seized approximately 11,500 litres of diesel worth RM58,880, along with an additional 1,200 litres found in an intermediate bulk container (IBC), valued at RM6,144.
"Eight fleet cards were also confiscated, believed to have been used by the syndicate for repeated refuelling before the fuel was gathered at the location," he said here today.
An IBC is a large, reusable container designed to transport and store liquids or granular substances in bulk.
Armizan said other items seized included an IBC tank containing diesel, fuel pumps, hose equipment, a trailer tank, a four-wheel-drive vehicle, a bonded lorry and four IBC units loaded onto a lorry.
"The total value of the seizure is estimated at RM265,986.48, with the diesel alone worth RM65,024.
"The three men — a Malaysian and two Indonesians in their 30s and 40s — were detained to assist in investigations under the Control of Supplies Act 1961," he said.
He added that the government would not compromise with any party involved in the diversion of controlled goods, including the abuse of fleet cards for illegal profit.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KPDN moves to fast-track prosecution of fuel smuggling offence
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) is streamlining its standard operating procedures (SOP) to expedite the prosecution of controlled goods misappropriation cases in court.
Its minister, Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali, said the move follows a decision by the Enforcement Agencies Coordination Supreme Committee on April 6, which emphasised the need to accelerate legal action under the Control of Supplies Act 1961.
He said the ministry has held discussions with the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC)and the Office of the Chief Registrar of the Federal Court, both of which provided positive feedback on expediting case management.
"The SOP improvements cover the handover process from other enforcement agencies to the KPDN Enforcement Division to ensure investigation papers are completed more quickly before submission to the A-GC.
"This approach ensures that investigations, prosecutions and court hearings are conducted more efficiently and systematically, allowing for swifter action against offenders," he said during a working visit to a petrol station in Taman Mount Austin here today.
Armizan said proposals include establishing fast-track trial channels, appointing dedicated judges, and coordinating case management between agencies to expedite case disposal.
"Priority will be given to cases involving cartels and syndicates, while a compound negotiation approach will be considered for minor cases.
"This aligns with the government's commitment to combat the diversion and smuggling of controlled goods, especially fuel, during the global crisis resulting from conflicts in the Middle East," he said.
In a related development, he said 362 cases involving 105 individuals have been recorded nationwide under Ops Tiris 4.0 (integrated) from March 16 until yesterday.
"During that period, 20,020 inspections were conducted, with diesel and petrol misappropriation recording the highest number of cases.
"Of the 362 cases, 157 involved diesel, followed by petrol (78), liquefied petroleum gas (49), cooking oil (42), sugar (26) and wheat flour (10)," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Duterte to face ICC trial after judges confirm charges
Article:
THE HAGUE: Former Philippines president Rodrigo Duterte will face trial at the International Criminal Court after judges on Thursday confirmed charges of crimes against humanity over his so-called "war on drugs."
Pre-trial judges "unanimously confirmed all the charges... against Rodrigo Roa Duterte and committed him to trial," the ICC said in a statement.
Duterte will be the first Asian former head of state to face trial at the ICC, which prosecutes individuals for the world's worst crimes such as war crimes and crimes against humanity.
The court faces the most difficult time in its 24-year history, with the United States sanctioning key judges and officials after the ICC issued arrest warrants for Israel's leader over the Gaza war.
It is unclear whether the 81-year-old Duterte will attend his trial.
His defence team says he is too weak mentally to follow proceedings and he did not appear at a week of hearings to assess the validity of the charges.
The only time he has been seen since his arrest was an initial appearance via video, where he seemed confused and tired, his speech barely audible.
The pre-trial judges concluded there were "substantial grounds to believe that Duterte is responsible for the crimes against humanity of murder and attempted murder," the ICC statement said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Defence Minister says police to investigate soldier in fatal accident
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin has confirmed he is aware of a fatal crash on the Maju Expressway (MEX) involving an allegedly intoxicated soldier.
However, he declined to comment further on the specifics of the incident, citing the ongoing police investigation.
"The matter should be handled by the police, despite the individual being from the military.
"Although it involves a soldier, we will let the police conduct the investigation. I cannot determine what actually happened," he told reporters after closing the Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security Asia (NATSEC) 2026 exhibitions at the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (MITEC) today.
It was reported that a 31-year-old soldier has been arrested on suspicion of driving under the influence of alcohol, leading to a
fatal accident on the MEX
early this morning.
The accident claimed the life of a 22-year-old Bangladeshi man and left four others, including the suspect, injured.
Initial investigations revealed that the suspect's car, travelling from Salak Selatan towards Jalan Tun Razak, veered into the opposite lane and crashed into an e-hailing vehicle.
The e-hailing vehicle was carrying two Bangladeshi passengers from Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) at the time of the collision.
The results of the breathalyser test on the driver of the car showed that the reading exceeded the permitted limit.
The driver has been arrested, and the case is being investigated under Section 44(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for driving under the influence of alcohol and causing death.
The offence carries a maximum prison term of 15 years and a fine of up to RM100,000.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three-tier strategy for dignified, graceful ageing
Article:
The National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 paints a grim picture of the state of affairs of our elderly.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad has pointed out that Malaysia is ageing 1.5 times faster than Japan. This rapid demographic shift is a ticking fiscal and social time bomb.
Thus, to ensure our seniors spend their last decade in dignity rather than in hospital beds, we need a concerted, three-tier strategy involving the state, the community and the individual.
Physical health is intrinsically linked to our environment. However, the Federal Park at Taman Tun Dr Ismail has been closed for upgrading for nearly two years. A lack of urgency in reopening public green spaces directly contradicts the government's call for healthy living. For a senior citizen, two years without a safe space to walk is a significant portion of their remaining mobile life.
Furthermore, we must move beyond ad-hoc programmes like the "90-day challenge" for civil servants. Health is not a sprint; it is a marathon. We need long-term evaluation and sustainability.
We must also mandate the private sector to integrate employee mental health and stress management into their ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) frameworks. Mental health is often the "silent passenger" in ageing that receives far less attention than physical non-communicable diseases (NCDs).
We need a district-level synergy where local councils, non-governmental organisations, and residents' associations jointly organise health and social activities. Ageing in grace is only possible when the community is designed to support it.
We cannot start talking about ageing at 60, or even 50. The foundation is laid in the classroom and at the dinner table. Parents and teachers must internalise the concept that exercise is not a "hobby" — it is a daily necessity as vital as eating and sleeping.
If the 30-to-60 age group does not prioritise preventive health now, no amount of government legislation will be able to support the healthcare burden they will pose in their senior years.
Consult all stakeholders, including the private sector and ordinary citizens, to develop a pragmatic roadmap. Focus on the "sandwich generation" (those aged 30 to 60) today to ensure they are the healthy seniors of tomorrow.
Preventive health is indeed the cheapest medicine, but it requires a culture shift. It is time for every Malaysian to realise that while the government provides the framework, the responsibility for a healthy old age is a shared national burden.
DR POLA SINGH
Kuala Lumpur
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Main suspect in Felda Kemahang shooting believed to have fled to Thailand
Article:
TANAH MERAH: Police believe the main suspect involved in the shooting of a Felda settler's son on Monday has fled to Thailand.
State police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said the suspect, a Malaysian believed to be involved in cattle smuggling, crossed the border shortly after the incident in Felda Kemahang here.
"We have identified the suspect, who is in his 40s. He is believed to have fled to (Thailand) by crossing the Golok River," he told reporters.
Yusoff said Kelantan police would seek assistance from their Thai counterparts to track down the suspect.
He added that police have so far arrested four individuals to assist in the investigation.
It was reported on Monday that a Felda settler's son, Mohd Khairul Amri Abdul Kadir, was wounded after he was
shot by a group of men
believed to be cattle smugglers.
In the 3am incident, the victim was with several friends when a group of four or five men entered the village and attempted to steal cattle.
The 42-year-old is currently warded in Tanah Merah Hospital.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Malaysia rejects external control of Strait of Malacca
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia has reiterated that any decision concerning the Strait of Malacca must be made collectively by littoral states, dismissing suggestions of external control over the strategic waterway.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the matter had already been addressed by Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan, stressing that regional consensus is paramount.
"Any action involving the Strait of Malacca must be agreed upon by countries in this region… Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia and Thailand," he told reporters after closing the Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security Asia (Natsec) 2026 exhibitions at the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (Mitec) today.
He emphasised that the strait remains safe and fully under the control of regional nations.
"There are no obstacles to vessels passing through, and there is no reason for any country, whether small or large, to express intentions to control it.
"The countries here are capable of managing it," he said.
Earlier,
Mohamad warned that
any proposal to impose fees for passage through the Strait of Malacca could disrupt global trade and jeopardise international relations.
Acknowledging Indonesia's position along the critical global trade and energy route, he said turning the strait into a source of revenue was neither simple nor appropriate.
"As an Asean member, and with the Strait of Malacca shared by several countries, any decision –  even those of strategic national interest – should be managed together to ensure we do not disrupt bilateral or multilateral ties," he told Buletin TV3.
"To impose any fee for free trade passage in the Strait of Malacca is a very significant decision and it would undermine world interests," he added.
Mohamad, commonly known as Tok Mat, said decisions regarding strategic interests along the route must be considered collectively, particularly within the Asean framework.
The remarks follow a suggestion by Indonesian Finance Minister Purbaya Yudhi Sadewa on the possibility of imposing fees on ships passing through the Malacca and Singapore straits.
However, Purbaya later withdrew the suggestion, clarifying that any such move would require the involvement of Malaysia and Singapore as fellow littoral states.
The 900km Strait of Malacca connects East Asia with the rest of Asia, the Middle East and Europe, handling about 25 per cent of global trade.
The route runs between Indonesia's Sumatra island and Peninsular Malaysia before narrowing into the Singapore Strait between Singapore and Indonesia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 17 injured in Danish train collision, five critical
Article:
HILLERØD, Denmark: Two commuter trains collided head-on near the Danish capital on Thursday injuring 17, of whom five were in critical condition, emergency services said.
"We can't provide any details for now about the cause of the accident," police official Morten Kaare Pedersen told reporters.
He said police were alerted to the accident at 6.29am (0429 GMT), which occurred about 40 kilometres (25 miles) north of Copenhagen between the towns of Hillerod and Kagerup.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IJM Corp eyes construction unit listing as early as next year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: IJM Corp Bhd may unlock value from its construction arm as early as next year, following the collapse of Sunway Bhd's takeover bid earlier this month.
Kenanga Investment Bank Bhd (Kenanga IB) said the group is studying a possible listing of its construction business, with a final decision expected within the next two months.
The move is part of a broader plan by IJM to unlock value across its businesses, for which a board-level committee has been set up.
"Management expects to decide within the next two months the final framework for a pure construction listing, which would carry a 12-month timeline," Kenanga IB said in a note.
On other asset unlocking initiatives, Kenanga IB said IJM's strategic exit from India involves a two-year window to optimise disposal gains.
With book value already reflecting market levels, the firm expects the divestment to be realised profitably without the risk of heavy impairments.
"The plan for a potential listing or business trust of local toll assets may take longer, as it requires authority approval regarding concession ownership.
"Consequently, a reasonable timeline to complete these asset unlocking exercises is approximately three years," it added.
Earnings from the company's construction business are expected to grow in financial year 2027 (FY27), supported by progress in its two major data centre projects in Pulai and Elmina.
Kenanga IB said IJM shares the view of other builders that are facing a "bearable impact" on margins from the war-induced rise in input costs.
"However, should the conflict persist for another three months, margins will likely compress further. Government projects remain shielded due to variation of price clauses, similar to cost-plus contracts, but fixed lump-sum contracts remain at risk if the war is prolonged," it added.
IJM's property unit registered a pre-tax loss of RM7.6 million in its nine-month period, dragged by higher operating costs in the United Kingdom and foreign exchange effects, while the Malaysian unit saw weaker sales.
However, property earnings are expected to improve in FY27, Kenanga IB said.
The firm maintained its "Outperform" call on the stock with a target price of RM3.40.
Sunway's takeover offer for IJM lapsed earlier this month after securing only 33.43 per cent acceptances, falling short of the required 50 per cent threshold.
The RM11 billion offer, announced in mid-January, proposed 31.5 sen in cash and 0.501 new Sunway shares for each IJM share, valuing the offer at RM3.15 per share.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia has potential for sovereign rating update: S&P Global
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia has the potential for a sovereign rating upgrade, supported by continuously narrowing fiscal deficits, S&P Global Ratings said.
Its managing director and sector lead for sovereign ratings, Kim Eng Tan, however, said an upgrade is unlikely in the near term due to the ongoing global supply crisis, despite a stable economic outlook.
He said this is due to Malaysia's external metrics, which measure foreign assets against foreign liabilities, appearing somewhat weaker than expected, given Malaysia's current account surplus.
"I think to some extent that reflects a lot of foreign investment in the domestic bond market, which is highly valued by many foreign investors, because they say that the Malaysian domestic bond market is very well developed compared to peers and so on.
"But one of the outcomes of that strong investment in Malaysian government securities is that it actually weakens the external metrics that we look at. Now, if these metrics were to improve, it could potentially, in a material and sustainable way, help to improve Malaysia's credit metrics," he said at the S&P-RAM Forum 2026 today.
Tan was a panellist in a session titled "From Tariff Shocks to Energy Shocks: Policy Making in a Multipolar Reality".
Last year, S&P reaffirmed Malaysia's sovereign credit rating at 'A-' with a stable outlook, despite significant uncertainty in global trade flows.
The agency said the performance reflected confidence in Malaysia's macroeconomic management, supporting stronger growth prospects than most sovereigns at similar income levels.
Meanwhile, Tan said the rapid growth of the Malaysian economy could support a future upgrade.
"If fiscal performance improves and the deficit starts coming down to below 2.0 per cent, and hopefully even below 1.0 per cent, then I would say there is a decent case for higher ratings," he said.
S&P Global Ratings and RAM Rating Services Bhd jointly hosted the forum, bringing together policymakers, investors and industry leaders to examine how markets are repricing risk amid heightened global uncertainty.
The event carried the theme "Pricing Risks, Seizing Opportunities", reflecting a shift in how capital market participants assess volatility, recalibrate strategies and position for growth.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gulf skirmish is squeezing the world's rubber
Article:
FOR weeks, the world's attention has been fixed on the chokepoint of the Strait of Hormuz. We've heard the grim forecasts: spiking oil prices, fertiliser shortages, and liquefied natural gas tankers idling at anchor.
But there is another, quieter victim of this Gulf skirmish, one that moves not in fiery pipelines but in the silent, steady rotation of a car tyre or the flex of a surgical glove.
The global rubber industry is about to be torn.
At first glance, rubber seems far removed from the sandbanks of the Gulf. Over 90 per cent of the world's natural rubber comes from the humid plantations of Southeast Asia — Thailand, Indonesia, Vietnam and Malaysia.
Not a single rubber tree grows near the conflict zone. So why should a skirmish 6,000 kilometres away cause a blowout in the rubber market?
The answer lies not in the growing, but in the getting there.
Rubber is a bulky, low-value-per-tonne commodity. The current restrictions on ship movements through the straits — whether from heightened insurance risks, naval patrols, or outright delays — are not just a local problem. They are a global logistics migraine.
Every day of delay in the Gulf sends ripples across the entire carrier scheduling network. Vessels that would normally carry Thai rubber to Rotterdam or Chinese manufactured tyres to Europe are being re-routed around the Cape of Good Hope, adding 10–14 days to each journey.
That ties up fleet capacity, reduces the number of available voyages per year, and — most critically — sends freight rates through the roof.
Spot container freight rates from Southeast Asia to Mediterranean ports have jumped in recent weeks, mirroring the spike in oil-driven bunker fuel costs.
For rubber shippers, this is a one-two knockout punch.
First, higher fuel surcharges. Second, a scramble for limited hull space as shipping lines prioritise higher-value goods like electronics or pharmaceuticals over ribbed smoked sheets or block rubber.
Then there is the matter of war risk insurance. Any vessel transiting the southern Red Sea or approaching the Gulf is now facing higher premiums.
And insurers, never fond of uncertainty, are adding clauses that exclude rerouting or delay coverage.
If a rubber-laden ship is held for inspection or waits out a threat, the cost of that idle time — demurrage — falls directly on the trader or buyer.
Consequently, many small and medium rubber traders are simply halting new shipments.
They cannot absorb the freight and insurance shock, and they cannot pass it all forward without killing demand.
This is creating a vacuum in the physical supply chain: rubber that has been harvested and processed in Sumatra is sitting in warehouses, while manufacturers in Europe, North America, and even China are starting to eye dwindling stocks.
So how will the world price of rubber respond? The short answer is with a sharp, jagged upward lurch, followed by extreme volatility.
The benchmark Tokyo Commodity Exchange (TOCOM) rubber futures have already climbed 18 per cent in the last fortnight, and that is likely just the opening salvo.
Why? Because unlike oil, rubber cannot be easily substituted in the short term. Yes, synthetic rubber exists — but it is made from crude oil derivatives (styrene, butadiene).
And oil prices are also spiking. The skirmish has thus created a rare double supply shock: natural rubber becomes expensive to ship, while synthetic rubber becomes expensive to produce.
Market watchers anticipate that within two months, global natural rubber prices will test earlier highs.
Not everyone loses equally. Large tyre multinationals with long-term supply contracts and diversified shipping arrangements will ride out the storm, albeit with thinner margins.
But the smallholder farmers, who produce nearly 85 per cent of the world's rubber, will see little of the price rise, as local middlemen will cite "logistical constraints" to keep farmgate prices depressed.
And the real pain will be felt by downstream manufacturers: medical glove factories in Malaysia, rubber parts makers in Germany, and retreaders in Brazil.
Their input costs are exploding just as consumer demand — already weakened by high oil and fertiliser prices — begins to buckle.
The skirmish in the Gulf is exposing the brittle, over-optimised nature of just-in-time commodity trade. For decades, the rubber industry assumed that ocean freight would remain cheap, predictable, and safe.
That era is now over for as long as the conflict lasts — and probably long after, as shippers permanently price in a "Gulf risk premium."
* The writer is a professor at the Tan Sri Omar Centre for STI Policy, UCSI University and Adjunct Professor, UAC, University Malaya
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL proposes water detention measures after Bukit Lanjan landslide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) has proposed an on-site water detention facility following the landslide in Kampung Bukit Lanjan.
Mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud said the move aims to ensure surface runoff, particularly in the affected area, can be properly controlled.
He said the proposal followed a site visit by his team.
"We conducted a site visit to review flood mitigation measures and the initial steps to be taken in the event of heavy rain.
"We also proposed designating a specific area for on-site detention to ensure water runoff can be managed more effectively," he said on the sidelines of the Baiti Jannati @ Wilayah Persekutuan handover ceremony today.
Water detention measures are stormwater management techniques that temporarily store runoff in basins or structures to reduce peak flow and prevent flooding and erosion.
The event was officiated by Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh.
Fadlun was responding to concerns raised by Kampung Bukit Lanjan residents following a landslide near their homes on Monday.
Earlier, images of the landslide, believed to have been triggered by heavy rain, went viral.
Residents said it was the first such incident in the area, particularly after parts of the surrounding forest were cleared and converted into a durian orchard five years ago.
A resident, who wished to be known only as Ahmad, said the incident was worrying as
landslides had never occurred there before.
"This is the first time. It is worrying for residents here," said Ahmad, who runs a food stall at the village entrance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Parents demand explanation over son's drowning
Article:
KUANTAN: A grieving couple is seeking answers following the tragic death of their son at Pantai Balok on April 17, raising questions over how a student residing in a school hostel ended up at the beach.
Luqman Kamil Jamaluddin, 16, was found drowned on April 18 after being reported missing while searching for 'kepah' (mussels) with several hostel mates at the beach at 6pm on April 17.
His father, Jamaluddin Wahudin, said many questions surrounding the incident remain unanswered, and the family has yet to receive any explanation from the school, the education department or the ministry.
"My son lives in the school hostel, so who provided the boys with transport to the beach? I never signed any documents allowing him to leave the premises, so how did they (students) even get past the school security post?
"It was alleged that the hostel warden's husband drove the boys to the beach. I know the school's security is extremely strict, as five of my children, including Luqman, studied there," he told reporters at his home in Felda Lepar Hilir 3 here today.
The 57-year-old factory operator said he found the chain of events leading to the drowning disturbing, especially as his son had allegedly gone to search for mussels.
"What would my son do with mussels when he has not even eaten them? My son could not swim.
"Luqman had even told me he would not be returning home for the weekend during the incident as it was study week for examinations and student outings were not allowed by the school," he said.
Jamaluddin said the family only wants the truth and justice for Luqman Kamil, the youngest of 10 siblings, who was a dormitory leader at school and a silat exponent.
"There were allegations that my son saved two friends from drowning before he went missing. We want to know the truth about what happened on the day of the incident," he said, adding that a police report was lodged on April 17.
His wife, Siti Roslina Misban, 56, described Luqman Kamil as a cheerful boy who often joked with his siblings and was the most pampered in the family.
"I have eight daughters and two sons, and Luqman is the youngest. I have been visiting his grave nearby every day.
"Life is different after Luqman's death, and I hope all our doubts will be answered soon," she said in a choked voice.
In the incident, Luqman's body was recovered at 2.15pm on April 18.
Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek said the ministry is
conducting
an internal investigation to determine whether there was any breach of standard operating procedures.
The Pahang Education Department said Luqman Kamil and the others had left the hostel for recreational activities without the school's knowledge, and that the outing was not part of an official school programme.
The New Straits Times has contacted Pahang Education Committee chairman Datuk Seri Mohd Soffi Abd Razak for comment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: L&G set for earnings rebound on strong development pipeline
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Land & General Bhd (L&G) is entering a phase of strong earnings recovery, supported by higher property progress billings and a solid development pipeline, according to TA Securities.
The research firm said that L&G has three new launches lined up for the financial year ending FY27, with a combined gross development value (GDV) of RM930 million.
A key focus is the Kerling industrial land in Hulu Selangor, a 1,012-hectare (ha) site undergoing rezoning to industrial use. The first phase, spanning 97.1ha and carrying an estimated gross development value of M1.5 billion, is planned for launch by year-end, with early interest from manufacturers and data centre players.
TA Securities estimates the land's value could rise from 44 sen per square foot (psf) to about RM20 psf after rezoning, supported by strong industrial demand in Selangor.
Operationally, L&G's earnings momentum has strengthened significantly. Core profit rose 280.5 per cent year-on-year to RM38 million in nine months of FY26 (9M FY26), already above recent full-year levels. Revenue grew 141.7 per cent to RM348.3 million, while margins improved to 16 per cent from 11 per cent.
The research house said earnings visibility remains solid, backed by RM637 million in unbilled sales, with about 80 per cent expected to be recognised in FY27.
Looking ahead, it expects earnings growth to continue into FY27 before Kerling contributes meaningfully from FY28, with profit forecast at RM52 million to RM83 million over FY26 to FY28 versus RM29.5 million in FY25.
L&G also has RM8.5 billion in undeveloped GDV and a low gearing level of 2.5 per cent as of the third quarter of FY26.
Meanwhile, its education division continues to provide steady income, contributing 10 per cent of revenue and 23 per cent of operating profit.
TA Securities values L&G at 20 sen per share, based on 0.5 times book value, noting FY26 could be an inflection point driven by stronger earnings visibility and asset re-rating potential.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sainsbury's echoes Tesco warning as Iran war weighs on outlook
Article:
LONDON: British supermarket group Sainsbury's warned on Thursday that uncertainty around how the Iran war will affect consumers is clouding its outlook and could drag profits lower this year, echoing concerns voiced by market leader Tesco.
Sainsbury's said the conflict in the Middle East will impact both its customers and its business.
"The duration and extent of these impacts is very uncertain and this is reflected in our profit guidance," it said, forecasting total underlying operating profit of between 975 million pounds and 1.075 billion pounds (US$1.32-US$1.45 billion) for its 2026/27 year.
Sainsbury's made retail underlying profit of 1.025 billion pounds in the year to February 28 2026, down 1.1 per cent on 2024/25 but in line with guidance.
"We have made a positive start to the new financial year, with grocery volume growth ahead of the market. Argos trading continues to reflect a subdued general merchandise market," it said.
About one quarter of Sainsbury's sales are non-food, making it more exposed than Tesco to any war-related slowdown in discretionary spending.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia receives US$60.9mil as ADB channels US$9bil into Southeast Asia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Asian Development Bank (ADB) committed US$9 billion to Southeast Asia in 2025, with Malaysia receiving the smallest share among the region's major economies.
According to ADB's 2025 annual report, US$7.3 billion was channelled to public sector finance, while US$1.6 billion supported private sector initiatives.
The Philippines emerged as the largest recipient in the region with US$6.81 billion, followed by Indonesia (US$4.39 billion), Vietnam (US$1.96 billion) and Thailand (US$1.45 billion).
Malaysia received US$60.9 million.
ADB said US$60.2 million of that amount was a private sector loan to Jingxing Holdings Malaysia Sdn Bhd for the expansion of its wastepaper recycling and packaging paper manufacturing facility in Selangor.
The bank said the financing would support the installation of advanced technology to lift the facility's annual production capacity by 600,000 tonnes.
ADB said its investments remain focused on strengthening Southeast Asia's long-term economic prospects, particularly through higher productivity, market-relevant skills development, and improved nutrition, education and healthcare outcomes.
It added that support for women and girls was further expanded, including assistance for women entrepreneurs, alongside efforts to strengthen public financial management and fiscal resilience to build more sustainable and inclusive economies.
Globally, ADB's total commitments in 2025 — including loans, grants, equity investments, guarantees and technical assistance — rose 20 per cent year-on-year to US$29.3 billion.
ADB president Masato Kanda said the figure was complemented by US$14.7 billion in co-financing from development partners.
"Of our commitments, US$23.5 billion was for programmes and projects in the public sector, while US$5.5 billion were directed to the private sector," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Khazanah flags scams misusing its name, branding
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Khazanah Nasional Bhd has urged the public to remain vigilant against fraudulent investment schemes falsely claiming to be linked to the sovereign wealth fund.
In a statement, Khazanah said scammers have been misusing its name, logo and branding across social media platforms and messaging applications to promote fake investment opportunities.
These schemes typically lure individuals with promises of quick and high returns, while spreading misleading and entirely false information.
The fund said it has also received reports of impersonation attempts on platforms such as Telegram, with scammers posing as its representatives.
In some cases, scammers have gone further by using images and identities of senior leadership to appear credible and convince victims to invest.
Khazanah governance, risk and compliance head Datuk Suhana Dewi Selamat said the fund does not engage in any form of public fundraising or retail investment services.
"As an institutional investor, our investments are anchored in delivering sustainable, long-term value and impact for the nation.
"We do not provide retail investment services such as unit trusts, mutual funds or investment platforms for individuals," she said.
Khazanah said it will not hesitate to pursue legal action and work with authorities against those who misuse its name and identity.
It urged the public to remain cautious by avoiding suspicious messages, not sharing personal information, and refraining from making payments to unknown individuals or entities claiming affiliation with the fund.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Clearing Hormuz Strait mines could take six months: Report
Article:
WASHINGTON: A Pentagon assessment said it could take six months to completely clear the Strait of Hormuz of Iranian-laid mines, which could keep oil prices high, the Washington Post reported on Wednesday.
Iran has all but blocked the vital waterway since the start of a war with the United States and Israel, sharply driving up oil and gas prices and disrupting the global economy.
The strait – through which one-fifth of the world's oil and gas passes in peacetime – has remained largely closed during a shaky ceasefire, with the US imposing its own blockade.
Even if hostilities end and the blockade lifts, it could take months to clear the waterway of mines, according to a Pentagon assessment, the Washington Post reported citing officials close to the discussion.
The assessment added that it was unlikely such an operation would begin before the end of the war.
The six-month estimate was shared with members of the House Armed Services Committee during a classified briefing, the Post reported.
Lawmakers were told that Iran may have placed 20 or more mines in and around the strait, some floated remotely using GPS technology which makes them harder to detect, according to the report.
AFP has contacted the Department of Defence for comment.
Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell told the Washington Post that its information was "inaccurate."
Iran's Revolutionary Guards have warned of a "danger zone" covering 1,400 square kilometres – 14 times the size of Paris – where mines may be present.
Iran's parliament speaker said the Islamic republic would not reopen the strait as long as the US naval blockade remained.
A spokesman for German transportation giant Hapag-Lloyd cautioned last week that shippers needed details on viable routes as they remain fearful of mines.
When the Hormuz strait briefly reopened at the start of the ceasefire this month, only a few ships trickled through amid fears of attacks or mines.
Earlier in April, the US Navy said its ships transited the waterway to begin removing the mines, but that claim was denied by Iran's Revolutionary Guard, which threatened any military vessels attempting to cross the channel.
London hosted talks with military planners from over 30 countries starting Wednesday on a UK and France-led multinational mission to protect navigation in the Strait of Hormuz once hostilities end.
The "defensive" coalition is set to discuss plans to reopen the strait and conduct mine clearance operations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC chief appointment must uphold integrity, public trust – MCW
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The appointment of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Chief Commissioner is not merely about an individual but concerns the credibility of a key institution in the fight against corruption, abuse, and misconduct.
Malaysian Corruption Watch (MCW) president, Jais Abdul Karim, stressed that any decision regarding the tenure or appointment of a new chief must be based on integrity, track record, and public confidence, not political pressure or perception.
"As highlighted by Istana Negara, this is a crucial position to ensure the MACC functions effectively and independently. It should not be politicised by any party.
"Any decision to either not renew a contract or make a new appointment must be rooted in integrity, performance and public confidence, not mere perception," he said in a statement today.
The remarks follow a statement by His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, who said he would choose the new chief commissioner to lead the agency.
The King emphasised that the role was vital in ensuring the MACC remains the nation's primary institution in the fight against graft and malpractice.
Addressing past allegations regarding share ownership linked to the current leadership, Jais said a transparent judicial process or conclusion should be announced to ensure fairness to all parties.
"In the context of enforcement bodies, even the shadow of a conflict of interest or controversy can undermine public trust in the entire system, even if not proven in court.
"If serious doubts regarding leadership integrity exist, independent and transparent review mechanisms must show they are functioning," he added.
Jais suggested that future appointments of the chief commissioner be more open, merit-based and accountable to Parliament.
He also called for the acceleration of institutional reforms, including the establishment of an independent appointments commission.
"In line with the King's decree that this matter should not be politicised, MCW urges all parties to stop using the MACC as a political narrative tool. It must be strengthened as a truly independent institution, has integrity and is feared by the corrupt," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CAP urges urgent action over prolonged Sg Ketil pollution
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Consumers' Association of Penang (CAP) has expressed serious concern over the continued pollution of Sungai Ketil in Gua Musang, Kelantan, saying the problem has persisted for more than five years despite repeated complaints and regulatory attention.
CAP said the ongoing contamination has caused significant ecological damage, with freshwater species such as kelah, baung and lampam among those most affected.
The decline in fish populations, it added, has directly affected local communities who depend on the river for both income and daily sustenance.
Residents believe the pollution is linked to industrial effluent discharge, with sections of the river turning black, frothy and emitting a strong foul smell.
CAP president Mohideen Abdul Kader said the association had repeatedly raised the issue with the Kelantan Department of Environment (DOE) and relevant local authorities, urging swift enforcement action.
However, he said the problem continues to recur, including a recent incident reported in Berita Harian on April 18, underscoring what he described as a lack of lasting intervention.
"The Environmental Quality Act 1974 must be enforced without compromise, including firm prosecution and heavier penalties against identified polluters.
"We urge a thorough and transparent investigation into the repeated failures to resolve this issue, and the findings must be made public for the benefit of affected communities," he said in a statement.
Mohideen said that continued inaction could lead to further deterioration of water quality in Sungai Ketil, potentially pushing key fish species towards local extinction and worsening the economic hardship of river-dependent communities.
He also cautioned that the pollution poses a potential public health risk if contaminated water is used for domestic purposes by nearby residents.
"We urge the Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Ministry to initiate a comprehensive and transparent investigation into the prolonged failure to resolve the issue, despite years of complaints and reports, and to ensure that enforcement actions are made public to restore confidence among affected residents," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Switzerland favours out-of-court settlement in Crans-Montana fire
Article:
BERN: The Swiss government said on Wednesday that it favours an out-of-court settlement involving the New Year's fire disaster at a bar in the Crans-Montana ski resort that killed dozens of party-goers, many of them young people, reported German Press Agency (dpa).
The Bern government has appointed economist and former canton politician Laurent Kurth as chairman of a round table that is intended to bring together victims, relatives, insurers, potentially liable individuals and authorities, according to a statement.
"Kurth has extensive experience steering highly complex processes involving many different stakeholders," the statement stated.
The aim of the round table is to "simplify dialogue and support those affected in drawing up out-of-court settlement solutions."
A fire broke out during a New Year's Eve party in the Bar Le Constellation on New Year's night.
A total of 41 people died, many of them young people and around 80 were injured, most of them seriously.
Spark-spraying party fountains set foam on the ceiling on fire.
Bar operators Jessica and Jacques Moretti and several city officials are being investigated on suspicion of negligent homicide, bodily harm and arson.
Contrary to requirements, the bar had not been inspected for fire safety since 2019.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Another drink-driving case: Soldier causes crash, killing Bangladeshi, injuring three others [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 31-year-old soldier has been arrested on suspicion of driving under the influence of alcohol, leading to a fatal accident on the Maju Expressway (MEX) early this morning.
A 22-year-old Bangladeshi man was killed, while four others, including the driver, were injured in the two-vehicle collision, said Kuala Lumpur Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa.
"The results of the breathalyser test on the driver of the Ford Fiesta showed that the reading exceeded the permitted limit.
"The driver has been arrested, and the case is being investigated under Section 44(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for driving under the influence of alcohol and causing death," he said in a statement today.
The offence carries a maximum prison term of 15 years and a fine of up to RM100,000.
The incident occurred at 5.38am at Km1.9 of the expressway, on the stretch from Salak Selatan heading towards Jalan Tun Razak.
The suspect, who was driving a Ford Fiesta, is believed to have veered into the opposite lane and collided with a Perodua Alza operating as an e-hailing vehicle travelling on the left lane.
The e-hailing vehicle was carrying two Bangladeshi passengers from Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) at the time of the collision.
The 22-year-old male Bangladeshi passenger, who was seated in the middle row, sustained severe head injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene.
A 20-year-old female Bangladeshi passenger suffered injuries and is receiving treatment at Kuala Lumpur Hospital.
The e-hailing driver sustained head injuries, while a 36-year-old female passenger in the Ford Fiesta was also injured.
The soldier sustained minor injuries. All victims were taken to the same hospital for treatment.
Police also conducted a drug test on the suspect, with the urine test returning a negative result.
"Further investigations are under way, including obtaining closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage from the scene to assist in the investigation," Zamzuri said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Level 1 hot weather alert issued for 10 areas in Perlis, Kedah and Kelantan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) today issued a Level 1 hot weather status or alert category for 10 areas involving Perlis, Kedah and Kelantan.
MetMalaysia in a statement announced that the areas involved are the entire state of Perlis while in Kedah it involves the areas of Pokok Sena, Padang Terap, Pendang and Sik.
For Kelantan, the areas are Machang, Kuala Krai, Pasir Mas, Jeli and Tanah Merah.
According to MetMalaysia, Level 1 or alert status is issued when the daily maximum temperature in an area reaches 35 to 37 degrees Celsius for at least three consecutive days.
The public can refer to the department's official website to get the latest information on the daily hot weather status nationwide. — Bernama

Title: Police bust homestay hopping syndicate in Sarawak’s biggest drug haul to date
Article:
KUCHING, April 23 — A drug syndicate’s tactic of renting homestay premises as packaging and storage centres has been uncovered after police arrested four individuals and seized various types of drugs worth about RM16.8 million in two separate operations around Kuching this month, making it the biggest seizure recorded in Sarawak to date.
Sarawak Police Commissioner Datuk Mohamad Zainal Abdullah said that the first case involved a raid on a homestay in Santubong on April 7, which led to the arrest of a couple aged 41 and 31.
He said police also seized various types of drugs believed to be Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) weighing 107.5 kilogrammes (kg), ketamine (1.3kg), Erimin 5 pills (180 grammes), ecstasy pills (102 grammes) and syabu (28 grammes).
“The total seizure is estimated at RM16.2 million, and the seized drugs could potentially supply up to 16,952 addicts,” he told a press conference at the Sarawak police headquarters here today.
He said the syndicate is believed to have been active since the beginning of this month and used several homestay premises as temporary locations for packaging activities before moving to other locations to avoid detection by the authorities.
Mohamad Zainal said the two suspects were believed to be involved in packaging and distributing drugs to locations designated by other syndicate members via WhatsApp.
“They were paid RM3,000 monthly plus a RM5 commission for every packet of drugs sold,” he said, adding that police also seized a vehicle worth an estimated RM15,000 and RM1,856 in cash.
He said the success was the result of an operation by the Bukit Aman Narcotics Crime Investigation Department (NCID) in cooperation with the Sarawak NCID.
Both suspects tested negative for drugs, while checks found that the woman has three prior criminal records.
The couple were charged in court at the Kuching Magistrate’s Court on April 20 with four counts under Section 39B(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, one count under Section 39A(1) and two counts under Section 12(2) of the same Act.
In the second operation on April 19, police arrested two men, aged 28 and 38, around Jalan Setia Raja, following information from the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (AKPS).
Inspection of the suspects led to the discovery of 1,281 packages of vape cartridges suspected of containing marijuana liquid, weighing an estimated 3.2kg and valued at about RM576,450.
“The two suspects are believed to have acted as runners and transporters within the syndicate network, receiving about RM8,000 for each delivery,” he said, adding that the case is being investigated under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
According to him, the two suspects have no prior records and also tested negative for drugs, while police also seized a vehicle worth about RM12,000.
In a related development, Mohamad Zainal said the “No Day Without Arrests” approach implemented by the Sarawak contingent has shown positive results, with 5,151 drug-related arrests recorded from January 1 to April 19 this year compared to 3,728 in the same period last year.
He said the value of drug and poison seizures for the same period surged to RM31.9 million compared to RM3.1 million last year, which is a 913.7 per cent increase. — Bernama

Title: Miri woman loses RM485,832 in Instagram scam
Article:
MIRI, April 23 — A local woman lost RM485,832 after falling victim to a fraudulent online investment scheme that promised quick returns.
Miri district police Chief ACP Mohd Farhan Lee Abdullah said the 48-year-old victim lodged a police report yesterday after realising she had been duped.
He said the victim was initially drawn to an investment advertisement on Instagram before contacting an individual identified as “Han Zhi Hai” via an international phone number.
“The victim was promised high returns within a short period and proceeded to make 14 money transfers to 12 different bank accounts, amounting to RM485,832, as instructed by the suspect,” he said in a statement.
Mohd Farhan said the victim grew suspicious after failing to receive the promised profits and was repeatedly given excuses, leading her to realise she had been scammed.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, which carries a jail term of between one and 10 years, as well as whipping and a fine, upon conviction. — Bernama

Title: Government freezes 163 unauthorised non-Muslim religious sites in KL, launches structured solution plan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — The government has imposed an immediate freeze on any new construction or expansion at 163 unauthorised non-Muslim places of worship (RIBI) identified on public land in the city.
Announcing the move, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said 11 new sub-committees, led by local members of parliament, will be established to seek fair and structured solutions for these existing sites.
The initiative follows a high-level meeting chaired by Yeoh, which for the first time included Kuala Lumpur MPs and the Malaysian Consultative Council of Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Sikhism and Taoism (MCCBCHST) to strengthen the resolution process.
“The Madani government is adopting a structured, prudent and fair approach to handling this matter, taking into account community needs and existing legal frameworks,” she said in a statement.
The Federal Territories Department (JWP) has completed a mapping exercise, identifying 163 unauthorised RIBI across various parliamentary constituencies.
To address the issue at a grassroots level, the 11 sub-committees will be formed, each chaired by the MP of the respective area. These committees will lead discussions with religious leaders and other relevant parties to find workable solutions for the existing structures.
Yeoh also said any attempts to build new structures or expand existing ones at these unauthorised sites will be subject to enforcement action by local authorities.
“Starting today, no construction or additions will be allowed,” Yeoh stated, but gave her assurance that all enforcement actions would be carried out “prudently and respectfully, taking into account local community sensitivities.”
The move is supported by a parallel effort at the national level, where the Ministry of Housing and Local Government is refining guidelines for the management of non-Islamic places of worship to ensure a more orderly approach nationwide.
Yeoh expressed confidence that this inclusive strategy would lead to positive outcomes.
“I believe this inclusive and prudent approach will ensure that every issue is resolved well while maintaining harmony in the multicultural society of the Federal Territories,” she said.

Title: Two former company directors charged over diesel investment scam, money laundering
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — Two former directors of an investment company were charged in separate Sessions Courts today with cheating in a diesel business investment scheme and receiving proceeds from illegal activities.
Shamsul Rijal Mohd Yussof, 74, and his former wife, Hamidah Ab Hamid, 62, both pleaded not guilty after all charges were read before Judges Azrul Darus and Norma Ismail.
Before Judge Azrul, Shamsul Rijal faced a charge of cheating one Afifi Ahmad, 49, by deceiving him into investing in a diesel business venture.
The accused allegedly promised lucrative profits, which led the victim to transfer RM725,000 into a bank account registered under the name of Spectrum Global Sdn Bhd at a bank in Brickfields at 2.39pm on February 26, 2024.
The charge was brought under Section 420 of the Penal Code, which carries a prison term of between one and ten years, along with caning and a possible fine upon conviction.
Shamsul Rijal is also facing a charge of receiving RM20,000 in proceeds from unlawful activities from the Spectrum Global bank account, cashed out through a bank account belonging to a 63-year-old woman.
The offence is alleged to have taken place at a bank branch on Jalan Tun Razak on March 15, 2024, under Section 4(1)(b) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities (AMLA) Act 2001.
A conviction under this section carries a prison sentence of up to 15 years, plus a fine set at no less than five times the value of the unlawful proceeds at the time of the offence, or RM5 million, whichever is higher.
Before Judge Norma, Shamsul Rijal and Hamidah were jointly charged with receiving RM122,000 in proceeds from unlawful activities from the Spectrum Global bank account, which was cashed out through a bank account belonging to Reach Resource Management Plt. at a bank branch in Taman Melawati on February 26, 2024.
Hamidah faces an additional charge of receiving RM130,000 in proceeds from unlawful activities from the Spectrum Global bank account into her bank account, said to have occurred at a bank branch in KL City Centre between March 6, 2024, and March 29, 2024. All charges fall under Section 4(1)(b) of the AMLA Act, carrying similar penalties.
The case was handled by deputy public prosecutors Nurliyana R. Azmi and Muhammad Zul Hilmi Latif, while lawyer Farhanna Roslan represented Shamsul Rijal. Hamidah was unrepresented.
The court allowed Shamsul Rijal bail of RM50,000 with one surety, and RM20,000 bail for Hamidah. The next case mention has been set for June 8. — Bernama

Title: Melaka police chief: Toddler tested positive after accidentally ingesting suspected drug-laced sweets
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — A three-year-old girl is believed to have tested positive for drugs after consuming sweets suspected of containing narcotic substances, which were allegedly purchased by her social media influencer father.
Melaka police chief Deputy Commissioner Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said the 39-year-old suspect, who has nearly 600,000 online followers, has been arrested in connection with the case,
The Star
reported.
The matter came to light on April 14 after a medical officer at Hospital Melaka treated the child, who was brought in in a semi-conscious state.
Initial investigations revealed that the sweets were purchased by the suspect in a neighbouring country. He then stored them in a refrigerator at his mother’s house in Sungai Putat, who is also the child's caregiver.
"The child, who is fond of sweets, consumed them before losing consciousness and was then rushed to the hospital," Dzulkhairi said in a statement today.
He added that the sweets in question were not found during a police inspection of the house.
Media reports on Wednesday confirmed that the child tested positive for Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), the main psychoactive compound found in cannabis.
She has since recovered and has been discharged from the hospital.
Following the incident, a team from the Melaka police’s Sexual, Women and Child Investigation Division (D11) arrested the suspect at about 9.30pm on April 15.
Dzulkhairi confirmed that the suspect has no prior criminal record, and his own urine screening test came back negative for drugs. He has been remanded to assist in the ongoing investigation.

Title: Mat Sabu: Malaysia-Vietnam ties key to stable food supply amid global shortages
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — Agriculture and Food Security Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu says close Malaysia-Vietnam ties are vital to ensure the country's food supply remains stable amid global shortages triggered by the West Asia conflict.
On his working visit to Vietnam, he said the trip aims to ensure the neighbouring country remains a steady and reliable trading partner for food supplies to Malaysia, including rice and other key commodities.
“This visit is especially significant as the world grapples with a global supply crisis that's disrupting food chains and price stability.
“In this situation, I see the need for Malaysia to take a more proactive, strategic, and forward-looking approach,” he said in a Facebook post today.
Mohamad expressed his commitment to bolstering global partnerships, broadening supply sources, and safeguarding the nation's food security.
“Insya-Allah, I'll keep working to ensure our nation's food supply stays stable, sufficient, and secure for everyone's wellbeing,” he said.
Earlier, Mohamad was welcomed in Hanoi by Malaysia's Ambassador to Vietnam, Datuk Tan Yang Thai, and his wife, Datin Akiko Morioka Tan. — Bernama

Title: Sabah to take over environmental management powers under MA63 starting November, says Musa Aman
Article:
KOTA KINABALU, April 23 — Sabah is set to receive a transition of power regarding environmental management under the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63), which is scheduled to be fully implemented in November, said Yang Dipertua Negeri Tun Musa Aman.
He said it will begin with the management of scheduled waste, and the move will be supported through efforts to draft new laws and guidelines.
“This also includes the development of a web-based management system as well as a programme to improve the technical competence of officers and industry players,” he said when delivering the state government policy speech at the opening of the First Meeting of the 17th State Legislative Assembly here today.
Also present were Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor, State Assembly Speaker Datuk Seri Kadzim M Yahya and the deputy speakers.
Tun Musa said that in terms of carbon governance, the state government aims to be a leader in implementing Article 6 of the Paris Agreement in Malaysia by leveraging voluntary carbon market opportunities as a new source of funding to support forest and environmental conservation efforts.
He said that in terms of land and property governance, the state government will table an amendment bill to the Land (Subsidiary Title) Enactment 1972 this year.
He said this involves the drafting of two new enactments, namely the Land (Subsidiary Title) Enactment and the Building Management Enactment, which aim to increase efficiency in the issuance of subsidiary titles as well as building management and maintenance in Sabah.
The Forest Landscape Restoration (FLR) approach will also be implemented, covering agricultural land, production forests and community areas, to restore ecological functions and improve the social and economic well-being of local communities.
Musa said that under the 13th Malaysia Plan, the state government will continue to intensify efforts in forest conservation and protection, strengthen forest and climate governance and promote community development and green innovation.
“Among the initiatives implemented are the Other Effective Area-Based Conservation Measures (OECM) to support the target Totally Protected Areas (TPA), the implementation of the Sabah Timber Legality Assurance System Plus, as well as the use of satellite technology and Earth Observation systems,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Three men arrested after police uncover 5,111 cartons of untaxed cigarettes in Seri Kembangan home
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 23 — Police arrested three men and seized 5,111 cartons of various brands of cigarettes, believed to involve RM1.281 million in unpaid customs duties, in a raid under Op Taring Alpha 1 on a house in Taman Sri Serdang, Seri Kembangan, Selangor, yesterday.
The Internal Security and Public Order Department’s Wildlife Crime Bureau/Special Intelligence Investigation (WCB/PSK) commander, Datuk Mohd Zaki Ashar, said the suspects, a supervisor and two workers, were aged between 34 and 43.
“The operation has successfully crippled the syndicate involved in the storage, distribution and sale of uncustomed cigarettes around the Klang Valley and abroad,” he said in a statement today.
Mohd Zaki said the cigarettes were found throughout the house, including the living room, bedrooms and storage areas.
He added that the suspects and seized items had been handed over to the investigating officer from the Criminal Investigation Division of the Serdang Police Headquarters, and that the case is being investigated under Section 135(1)(d) of the Customs Act 1967. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia to target fuel smugglers with designated judges and fast-track trials
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 23 — The government is moving to expedite court proceedings for cases involving the smuggling of controlled goods, particularly petrol and diesel, to send a firm signal that it will not compromise on leakages that threaten national supply.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali said the decision was made at a high-level inter-agency meeting on April 6, with the goal of strengthening enforcement from investigation right through to prosecution and sentencing.
“We want the responsible parties to be brought to justice as soon as possible,” Armizan told reporters after visiting a petrol station here today.
“This will ensure the continuity of supply for the people and the market.”
The initiative has already received a positive response from the Attorney General’s Chambers and the Federal Court’s registry office.
Armizan explained that his ministry has streamlined its standard operating procedures (SOPs) to ensure investigation papers are completed more quickly.
Proposals have also been put forward for improvements at the judicial level, including creating a fast-track channel for smuggling cases, appointing designated judges to hear these cases, and coordinating case management teams between agencies.
“These are proposals to assist in expediting the court process and subsequently the disposal of cases within a reasonable period,” he said, adding that priority would be given to large-scale syndicate or cartel cases.
For minor offences, targeted approaches like plea bargaining may be used.
Armizan stressed that this aggressive action is a direct response to the global energy and supply crisis triggered by the ongoing conflict in West Asia.
